summarization.a: Number of F and C atoms in the SiO₂ sample versus CF₂⁺ ion impacts at 20 eV. F accumulates to ~100 atoms and saturates after ~250 impacts, while C remains low (~15 atoms), producing a strongly F-rich passivation layer.

a.question: What is the approximate F/C ratio in the passivation film at steady-state?
a.answer: Approximately 6–7 (F ~ 100, C ~ 15), indicating a strongly F-rich film.

a.question: Why does the fluorocarbon film become F-rich at 20 eV?
a.answer: At energies above 10 eV, carbon is sputtered more easily from the surface than fluorine because the F–Si bond is stronger than the C–Si bond. This preferential C removal results in a passivation layer enriched in fluorine.

a.question: After how many impacts does the film composition reach quasi-steady-state?
Does increasing the number of impacts beyond 300 significantly change the film composition?
a.answer: No, the composition has already saturated.
